original_abstract:
Diffusion Imaging in Python (Dipy) is a comprehensive, free, and open-source software library designed for the analysis of diffusion magnetic resonance imaging (dMRI) data. As a key component of the Nipy ecosystem, Dipy provides a unified and transparent implementation of a wide array of dMRI algorithms, ranging from well-established classical methods to cutting-edge research techniques. The library includes robust tools for signal pre-processing, such as brain masking and gradient table management, and supports a diverse set of reconstruction models, including Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI), Diffusion Spectrum Imaging (DSI), Q-ball Imaging (QBI), and Constrained Spherical Deconvolution (CSD). 

Dipy also offers advanced capabilities for deterministic and probabilistic fiber tractography, as well as an extensive suite of post-processing tools for streamline clustering (notably the QuickBundles algorithm), connectivity analysis using regions of interest (ROIs), and 3D visualization. Built on a high-performance stack of Python libraries like Numpy, Scipy, and Cython, Dipy is engineered for computational efficiency and code readability, making it accessible to both clinicians and developers. Unlike many proprietary or single-institution tools, Dipy is an international, community-driven project that emphasizes collaborative development, rigorous testing, and reproducible research in the field of white matter imaging.
humanized_abstract:
Diffusion Imaging in Python (Dipy) is an open-source software library that provides a comprehensive set of tools for analysis of diffusion magnetic resonance imaging (dMRI) data. As a core component of the larger Nipy project, Dipy brings together, in a simple and transparent way, all available diffusion MR analysis methods, from well established classical techniques to advanced research methods. This includes, but is not limited to, pre-processing methods such as brain masking and gradient table handling, as well as reconstruction models ranging from classical Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI), Diffusion Spectrum Imaging (DSI), and Q-ball Imaging (QBI) to more advanced techniques such as Constrained Spherical Deconvolution (CSD).

Dipy has been designed to facilitate analysis of diffusion MRI in the framework of brain connectivity, as well as to provide advanced tools for the deterministic and probabilistic diffusion tractography and a wide range of post-processing tools for streamline clustering (for instance using the QuickBundles algorithm), analysis of white matter connectivity with regions of interest, and 3D visualization. By leveraging a stack of high-level, performance-oriented Python libraries, including NumPy, SciPy and Cython, Dipy strikes a good balance between performance and readability, and makes diffusion MRI analysis accessible to both clinicians and developers. Unlike proprietary or single-institution developed software, Dipy is an international community-driven effort focusing on collaborative software development, comprehensive testing and reproducible research.
